course_description: Empirically based analysis of the behaviour of consumers, firms and the economy as a whole, emphasizing the effects of complexity and incomplete and dispersed knowledge on resource allocation. The roles played by rules, networks and institutions in economic systems. Economic relevance of chaos and complexity theory.
timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: This course aims to develop a pluralistic approach to learning about economics by introducing the literature of behavioural and evolutionary economics and contrasting its philosophical and policy perspectives with those of mainstream economics taught in other courses.

learning_outcomes.description: Analyse economic problems in situations where consumers and managers have to deal with problems of information and knowledge.
Make better informed appraisals of economic policy discussions than you would have done if you had only studied mainstream economics.
Explain how behavioural and evolutionary approaches to economics constitute a bridge between economics, marketing, management and complexity sciences.

Employ an better understanding of the philosophical problems that underlie decision-making to both your own decision-making processes and to make informed appraisals of the quality of theories of decision-making offered by mainstream economists.
Explain how the 'moral dimension' shapes decision-making processes and identify situations in which consumers are most at risk of being duped by business policies.
Explain how behavioural and evolutionary approaches to economics differ from mainstream economic thinking and why they have taken so long to achieve a mass audience.

assessment_summary.title: Why Bounded Rationality?: The Short Version
The Evolution of the Photography Industry

assessment_details.title: Why Bounded Rationality?: The Short Version
assessment_details.task_description:
In the Learning Resources part of ECON2060's Blackboard site you will find a Word file of John Conlisk's paper 'Why Bounded Rationality? The Much too Long Version', which was published in P. Earl (ed.) (2000)
The Legacy of Herbert Simon in Economic Analysis

(Cheltenham, Edward Elgar). The word count for this paper is 27598 words. A shorter version, but still a long paper, appeared in the 1995
Journal of Economic Literature

(pp. 669-700). You task is to produce a much shorter version of this paper (with your own name replacing Conlisk's on the front page) of no more than 3000 words including references and footnotes. You can use as much of Conlisk's original wording as you like, with no fear of being accused of plagiarism. However, you should note that effective editing often requires rewriting, rather than merely removing some sentences, paragraphs or words.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

Submission:

To be submitted to Peter Earl in electronic form as a Word attachment to an e-mail message.

assessment_details.title: The Evolution of the Photography Industry
assessment_details.task_description: Examine the evolution of the photography industry in the light of theoretical material from this course. As you do so, give special attention to the role of Kodak in this industry and the change from film-based photography to digital imaging.

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description: The final exam will have three sections, each of which will consist of three essay questions. One question will have to be answered from each section. All questions will be equally weighted. Some questions may contain multiple sections.

learning_resources.required_resources: There is no required text for this course, since nothing has yet been written that covers the literature we use in teaching it. An extensive list of articles and book chapters is provided instead and you will be advised in lectues which are the key sources to consult. The learning activities show which readings particularly relate to which topic. You are not expected to read all the items listed but you should be aiming to read the equivalent of about three articles or chapters each week on average. Members of the class will receive a free copy of Peter Earl's book Lifestyle Economics , from which quite a lot of the first half of the course has evolved.

learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://library.uq.edu.au/search/r?SEARCH=ECON2060 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
SCHOOL OF ECONOMICS CALCULATOR POLICY

The School of Economics Calculator Policy (updated 8 January 2008) states that from Semester 1 2008, Casio FX82 series (82 with any letters) are the ONLY calculators permitted to be used in School of Economics examinations, both mid semester and final examinations. Any other calculator brought into the examination room will be confiscated.

PLEASE NOTE: This policy applies to all students enrolled in ECON coded courses.

REFERENCING GUIDELINES

Guidelines for correct referencing techniques can be found in the School of Economics publication Guide for Assignment Presentation , which can be accessed on the web site www.uq.edu.au/economics

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: L1 Introduction (Lecture): Introduction to the distinctive research approaches of behavioural and evolutionary economists. Bounded rationality, the evolution of rules and the survival of the 'sufficiently fit' Readings/Ref: Alchian1950; Earl1986 (ch. 1); Earl1995 (chs 2 and 6); Earl&W2005 (section 1.4); Metcalfe2008; Sent2004; Tomer2007; Veblen1898

L2 Cognition and Choice (Lecture): Instead of presuming that consumers 'know what they want and know how to get it', behavioural and evolutionary economics examine how consumers come to be looking for particular kinds of products and how they make assessments of them. These issues will be examined with particular attention to Hayek's 'The Sensory Order' and Kelly's 'Psychology of Personal Constructs' Readings/Ref: Earl1986 (chs 3, 4, 6); EarlPotts2004; Earl&W2005 (sections 2.9 and chapter 4); Earl&W2007; Frank2007; Gigerenzer1996

T1 Behavioural Economics/the History of Economics (Tutorial): Can the basic ideas of behavioural economics help explain why behavioural economics has taken so long to be taken seriously, and why there is a division between the 'new' and 'old' variants of behavioural economics? Readings/Ref: Altman2006; Sent2004; Tomer2007; Veblen1898; Wilkinson2008

L3 Lifestyles and the Evolving Consumer (Lecture): This lecture extends the previous one by exploring how the challenges that consumers face have been changing with economic development and how they change as consumers progress through their lives. A particular focus is on complementarities between what people consume, that are central to the notion of 'lifestyle'. We will also consider choices involving the 'moral dimension'. Readings/Ref: Earl1986; Earl&W2005 (ch. 4); Earl&W2007; EarlWakeley2009; Potts2000

T2 Behavioural Economics of the Family (Tutorial): What lessons does behaviorual economics have for understanding how people choose their partners/spouses, the number of children they have and how to raise them, and how people choose when to terminate a relationship? Readings/Ref: L&S2008; Potts2000 (chapter 6)

L4 Alternative views of Context and Choice (Lecture): The important of the context of choice is seen differently in 'old and 'new' approaches to behavioural economics. The 'new' version focuses on how the way that choices are presented is leads to 'heuristics and biases' having adverse impacts on choice, whereas the 'old' approach explores how people cope surprisingly well when faced with major challenges due to problems of information and knowledge. Readings/Ref: Altman2006; Belsky1999; Earl2007; EarlKemp1999; Gigerenzer1999; Heiner1983; Thaler1980; T&S2008; Wilkinson2008

T3 Behavoural economics and the environment (Tutorial): We will discuss the implications of behavioural economics for how the economy deals with environmental/ecological issues and implications for policy design Readings/Ref: T&S2008

L5 Coping with Uncertainty (Lecture): This lecture focuses on behavioural theories of how people cope with difficult decisions, particularly those where they face uncertainty and moral challenges. After examining 'Prospect Theory' and 'Regret Theory' quite briefly, we move on to consider Shackle's 'Potential Surprise' theory and other non-probabilitistic approaches Readings/Ref: Alchian1950; Earl1986 (chapter 8); Hanson1999a; Keynes1937; Thaler1980

T4 Information, Context and Choice (Tutorial): A discussion of the following questions: (1) Critically evaluate the design of the Amazon.com website in the light of material so far covered in this subject. (2)Why does economic theory lead you to expect differences in the context of choice to result in difference between the ways that consumers reach decisions when: (i) Shopping for weekly groceries (ii) Renovating a bathroom or kitchen (iii) Buying a CD player/tuner to replace their cars old radio-cassette player (iv) Choosing motels at which to stay whilst touring around on a motoring holiday (v) Buying their first mobile phone (vi) Purchasing legal services to obtain a property settlement with a former spouse.

L6 Behavioural & Evolutionary Approaches to Firms (Lecture): This lecture examines pioneering ideas of Nobel prize winter Herbert Simon, and colleagues Richard Cyert and James March on how business organizations work and what this implies for economic analysis of the firm. Loasby's capabilities approach to firms is also examined. Readings/Ref: CyertMarch1963; Earl1995 (chapter 6 and section 7.10); Earl&W2005 (sections 5.4, 6.3, chapter 8 and section 11.2); Leibenstein66; Loasby 1998; N&W1982; Simon59; Simon1979; Simon 1991

T5 Behavioural Economics/Entertainment Industry (Tutorial): We will explore the peculiarities of the movie and recorded music sectors and examine the extent to which behavioural/evolutionary economics can help us to understand the way these areas of business operate. Readings/Ref: DeVany2004; Earl2001

L7 Markets & Pricing Decisions (Lecture): This lecture examines the Marshallian tradition in pricing, which focuses on mark-up decision rules and the firm's need for customer 'goodwill'. It also examines the evolution of markets as sets of institutions to facilitate coordination. Readings/Ref: Andrews49; Bromley1997; CyertMarch1963 (chapter 8); Earl1995 (chapter 9); Earl&W2005 (sections 2.10 and 2.11, chapter 7 and section 9.6); Shove1942

T6 Australia's Consumer Policy Framework (Tutorial): The Productivity Commission recently published the Report of its Inquiry into Australia's Consumer Policy Framework. We will discuss its conclusions in the light of behavioural economics. Readings/Ref: Earl2007; Hanson1999a; PC2007; PC2008

L8 Creativity, Capabilities & Product Development (Lecture): This lectures integrates theoretical material on choice, cognition and knowledge from previous classes to understand how firms keep coming up with new products in the face of competitive pressure.

T7 Marshall's firm and evolution (Tutorial): We will consider the following two questions: (1)Critically assess Marshalls concept of the representative firm and explain why it may still be useful in the analysis of industrial competition. (2)What is evolutionary about Marshalls theory of the industry and competition?

L9 Productivity Growth and Structural Change (Lecture): This lecture examines industrial dynamics with a focus on how business operations with differing levels of efficiency can co-exist in a given market, and how the vertical division of labour between firms changes as markets evolve. Readings/Ref: Baily etal1992; Downie1958; Earl1995 (chapter 7); Earl&W2005 (section 6.3 and 11.4); L&R1995; Nightingale1996; Nightingale1997; Richardson 1972; Salter1966

T8 Evolution of Electonic Music Products Suppliers (Tutorial): We will discuss the modern-day relevance of Marshall's view of the firm, and the significance of Schunmpeterian creative destruction, in the context of the electronic music industry. Case companies to be focused on include: Fairlight (see http://www.fairlightau.com/), WEM (see http://www.watkinsguitars.co.uk/history.htm http://www.wemwatkins.co.uk/ http://www.wemwatkins.co.uk/) and (Jim, not Alfred) Marshall (see http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marshall_Amplifiers and http://www.marshallamps.com/heritage/first_30/first_30_01.asp)

L10 Pattern in Corporate Evolution (Lecture): This lecture examines the complex structures that emerge as firms grow and how the ways that firms grow is affected by the degree of environmental turbulence they face. Readings/Ref: Earl&W2007 (chapter 10); Kay1997; L&R1995

T9 The evolution of the global car industry (Tutorial Series): We will consider how behavioural and evolutionary economics can help explain the changing relative fortunes of major firms in the car industry, with a focus on the histories of General Motors, Honda and Kia. Please look these firms up on wikipedia and examine historical information on their websites. Readings/Ref: L&R1995 (chapter 4)

L11 Behavioural & Evolutionary Macroeconomics (1) (Lecture): This lecture will focus particularly on the behavioural underpinnings of business cycles and the role of financial innovation and changes in routines used in financial markets as drivers of the current global financial critis. Readings/Ref: A&S2009; EarlP&P2007; Mehrling1999; Pheby1989 (chapter 8)

T10 Structural change in the whitegoods sector (Tutorial): We will consider the following question: "In the light of theoretical material so far covered in this course, consider the growth of appliance maker Haier, China's first multinational corporation, and the challenges it poses for smaller local firms such as Fisher and Paykel'. You should read the wikipedia entries on both of their firms in preparation for the tutorial and follow the links to their websites.

L12 Behavioural & Evolutionary Macroeconomics (2) (Lecture): We will continue the previous lecture's analysis with a focus on the interplay between structural change and economic growth, and the economics of unemployment and inflation. Readings/Ref: Foster1987; Keynes1937; Mehrling1999; Pheby1989 (chapter 6); Schumpeter1928; Whalen2001; Wolfson2002

T11 Analysis of Financial Institutions (Tutorial): We will consider the following question: 'Discuss the case for analysing financial institutions from the standpoint of behavioural/evolutionary economics rather than in terms of mainstream approaches to choice, pricing and production."

L13 Globalization and Evolution/Overview (Lecture): This lecture examines the impact of globalization on structural change in the world economy in the light of the analysis of consumer and business behaviour presented in earlier lectures, before a concluding overview. Readings/Ref: Earl&W2005 (chapter 14); Friedman1999
T12 Revision queries/exam technique (Tutorial)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111 ).

Where an adjustment is made to an accredited program, it is the responsibility of the relevant Faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of the change/s.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
In the past decade there has been an explosion of interest in behavioural economics, largely based on work in the USA that has sought to refashion economic theory in the light of a growing body of findings that suggest actual behaviour is at odds with how 'rational economic man' is expected to behave. This new research has begun to influence policymakers and was, for example, considered in the recent Productivity Commission Report into Australia's Consumer Policy Framework. The new literature focuses particularly on the shortcomings of actual decision makers and largely ignores another body of research, dating back many decades, which emphasizes the difficulties that actual decision makers face when trying to choose and explores how they manage to cope, often rather well, in the face of these challenges. This course considers both the new and the older research on behavioural economics as it applies both to consumers and to firms. The older literature, whose key thinker Herbert Simon was awarded the Nobel Prize in 1978 for his work in this area, emphasizes the role of simple decision rules as a means for coping with complex situations. In this respect it has much in common with another long-established but often neglected body of thought, evolutionary economics, which focuses on economic processes and change rather than on equilibrium states, often making use of biological analogies in which decision rules are seen as serving a role rather like genes. Evolutionary economists are also concerned with complexity, but their emphasis in more on the structure of systems that make up the economy rather than problems of knowledge and information in decision making environments. The School of Economics at the University of Queensland has members of staff who have been making contributions in both behavioural and evolutionary economics for many years and those of its staff who work in these areas tend to bring the two approaches together as a whole. This course provides a unique opportunity to receive an introduction to this way of looking at economics.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Awarded for scores less than 20%.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Awarded for scores less than 45% but greater than or equal to 20%.

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

A common cause of students getting this grade on an answer, aside from having done little work or achieved a poor understanding, is a failure to answer the question that was set (for example, if the question says 'explore the relationships between the following concepts', you must explore the relationships between them, not define or describe them), or a failure to attempt all parts of the question. So, read the wording very carefully.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic requirement of being able to think like an economist is demonstrating that you consider the relative costs and benefits of alternative possible choices and the implications of differences in the incentives they offer to decision makers. If you fail to do this, you may have a hard time getting a 4, let alone a better grade, even if you look at the pros and cons of a particular choice from the standpoint of relevant economic theory.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Note here the phrase 'ability to apply': if your answers primarily report on facts that you have gathered or merely summarize the work of others you will be failing to show what you can do with economic concepts as tools of analysis and you will not score more than a 4. For a 5 of beyond, you must show that you can think like an economist rather than merely displaying abilities in terms of researching facts or what some economists think.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

To get beyond a 5, it is helpful to be able to demonstrate that you know what is predicted by relevant economic theory in the context in question and consider how well the predictions match up with facts.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Integrative thinking that relates concepts from different parts of the course will be highly regarded as will the application of ideas/arguments from reading beyond the prescribed text or lecture notes.
late_submission: A penalty of 1% from your final grade may be applied for each day an assignment is delivered late if an extension has not been approved.
other_assessment_information:
SPECIAL EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a special examination at a later time.

Information about special examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/special-examinations Please read this information very carefully.

All applications for special exams must be submitted within (5) days of the original date of the examination.

End of Semester Examination (CENTRAL exam) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at: http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/supplementary-examinations-2901 Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Special & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (16-18 December).
